focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By supper, with August's ten days still rasping in her ear, the dust had gone so fine it no longer drifted in visible threads. It lived in the house. It hid in the fold of the flour sack, under the butter crock lid, in the seam of the table oilcloth, and it rasped under Cora's knife as she pared potatoes at the sink. The window over the pump had the weak yellow look dust gave evening now. Even the light came in dirty.

Dell sat at the table with a dead alarm clock spread in a pie tin. He had lined the gears in a careful half-moon by size and kept touching their teeth with a burnt matchstick, not because that did anything Cora knew of, but because he could not bear a broken thing to lie in one helpless heap. He liked failure better when it came apart into named pieces. The clock case stood open by his elbow, nickel worn to a dull pewter, the little glass face clouded at one corner.

"You going to eat that raw or help me?" Cora asked.

He did not look up at once. "I'm looking for where it binds."

"The potato or the clock."

That got the corner of his mouth, not quite a smile. "Clock."

He was seventeen and all elbows until a machine was put in his hands. Then some steadier thing came over him. He bent close, dust-light hair falling toward the tin, and squinted with one eye shut, listening to a spring that had not made a sound in years. Cora watched him a second longer than she meant to. He had Ezra's long fingers and none of Ezra's ease. Even his patience showed strain; he had to hold it down with his shoulders.

Thea came in from the back step carrying the wash she had snatched from the line before dusk. Her dress had once been navy and now looked permanently weathered, the cuffs let down and rehemmed so neatly the mending showed only when she turned her wr...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...slid it back into the waxed paper. She chose a smaller sack for it instead of the drawer, one she could tuck under her apron if need be.

"Tomorrow at first light," she said. "You do the horse and chicken work before your mother wakes enough to count your footsteps. Then you come by the alley. Bring a spade if you can manage it without a speech from Cora."

He held her gaze a moment longer, face older now by the small amount a bad fact could age a person in one kitchen. "All right."

"And Dell."

"What?"

"Eat the rest of that bread. Investigations don't keep a boy upright."

He almost grinned then, and because he did not quite manage it, the expression hurt her more than if he had broken down. He took another bite obediently. Apple butter shone dark on the crust.

When he left by the back door a minute later, he carried his shoulders differently, as if the weight had shifted but not lightened. Hester watched through the flour-fogged pane while he cut across the alley toward the side street, head down against a wind too small to deserve the name and still full of grit enough to sting.

Then she turned back to her table, to the row of jars that had stopped being foolish in the dark and the dough that still had to be punched down before breakfast trade. The practical work remained. That was almost a comfort.

She set Otto's map beside the sponge crock where she could reach it, rolled up her sleeves, and drove both fists into the risen dough. It gave way warm and alive under her knuckles, breathing out sour sweetness and trapped air. Outside, light climbed the alley fence and the backs of the boarded stores toward Main Street. She punched again and pictured dawn laid out plain: Dell in the alley with a spade, the map under her apron, the south road before the bank opened.
